text: We here demonstrate that copper oxide derived surfaces exhibit selective CO2 reduction activity that correlates with the presence of metastable high CO-binding surface structures. Oxide-derived Cu (OD-Cu) surfaces prepared from H2-reduction of Cu2O precursors catalyzed the reduction of CO2 into acetate and ethanol with close to 50% Faradaic efficiency at relatively low overpotential. CO temperature-programmed desorption (TPD) on OD-Cu revealed the presence of distinct high-CO-binding sites that were not comparable to those present on terraces and steps of polycrystalline Cu foil, which have also been previously reported in solution studies. Following annealing at 350 °C, OD-Cu exhibits a 44 × lower surface area corrected current density for CO2 reduction along with <5% Faradaic efficiency for alcohol production. These observations correlate with an increase in population of lower CO-binding strengths. We propose that strong CO-binding sites on the OD-Cu surface are catalytic sites for selective CO2 reduction to products and that the identification of these catalytic sites constitutes a fundamental advance in this field.
explanation: The passage contains specific experimental details, measurements, and a coherent scientific narrative with a conventional abstract tone, which is more characteristic of human-authored literature than generic AI text.
